title: Year Two

Macroeconomics





Assignment
Analysed economy-wide phenomena including GDP measurement, business cycles, inflation, unemployment, monetary/fiscal policy, IS-LM framework, aggregate supply/demand and growth theory.
Evaluated macroeconomic models and policy debates through problem sets and current events analysis.
Grading: 40% assignments, 60% exam.

title: Investment Portfolio Management
text:
Initial Portfolio
For my initial portfolio, my main objective was to diversify across asset classes and minimise correlation between holdings. I began by selecting 32 assets from a range of sectors and industries, using five years of quarterly price data (up to 19 May 2025). This horizon provides a balance between the short, two‑month assignment period and a realistic long‑term investment perspective. After further research, I removed assets with high correlations to others and those whose risk did not seem justified by their expected return.

Risk: 🟢🟡🟠🔴⚪  Volatility: 🟢🟡🟠🔴🔴

A key challenge at this stage was adapting the dataset for companies with less than five years of history, as well as adjusting the risk‑free rate to correctly calculate the Sharpe ratio.
The initial asset allocation was:

Bonds – 15%
BNDX was included to reduce volatility and provide more stable growth.
ETFs – 25%
VOO is the largest ETF holding, offering diversified exposure to the 500 largest US companies, with a heavy tilt towards the technology sector.
Stocks – 30%
This bucket includes individual companies from a variety of sectors. I intentionally avoided pure technology names because VOO already provides significant exposure to this area. I also excluded stocks such as JNJ and PFE due to uncertainty around US healthcare policy.
Cryptocurrency – 6%
Despite its high volatility, I allocated a small portion of the portfolio to BTC as a high‑risk, high‑reward asset.
REITs – 10%
REITs help protect the portfolio against inflation but can be more volatile, with prices reacting quickly to market news.
Cash (USD, GBP, EUR) – 14%
Holding cash allows me to react to new opportunities and maintain liquidity. Limited exposure to EUR and GBP also helps manage currency risk for assets traded in these currencies.

At this point, the Sharpe ratio was 0.61, indicating clear room for improvement. Using Excel’s Solver, I experimented with different allocations but found that unconstrained optimisation tended to concentrate weights in one or two high‑volatility assets. To address this, I imposed realistic limits on asset and sector weights and refined the portfolio with a mix of quantitative results and qualitative judgement.
My initial shortlist combined both random and informed choices, with the goal of achieving broad sector and geographic exposure. I then constructed a correlation matrix and removed assets that were highly correlated with others (for example, Amazon and Nvidia), and ensured that no single sector was overweighted. In practice, this meant avoiding additional technology securities beyond those already captured through VOO, which still left the portfolio roughly 22% exposed to the technology sector.
I also excluded some stocks due to limited available information or because they operate in sectors undergoing significant structural change in the US. BTC was retained to maintain at least a small exposure to the cryptocurrency market in case adoption accelerates.
In terms of objectives, my realistic target was an annualised return of around 8% with a standard deviation below 10%. I initially worked with quarterly data to better reflect short‑term behaviour and to capture correlations more precisely, but I later converted the figures to an annual basis. This change increased the Sharpe ratio from 0.61 (quarterly) to 1.34 (annual), with an expected return of 17.81% and a standard deviation of 12.58%. This meant that my targets for return and Sharpe ratio were being met, but volatility was still slightly above my preferred level.

title: Investment Portfolio Management
text:
Week 2

Portfolio value: 100,495.07 USD (−0.51%)
Top performer: PLD – Prologis (+2.00%, +153.36 USD)
Worst performer: TSLA – Tesla, Inc. (−5.57%); RHM.DE – Rheinmetall AG (−258.20 USD)


Risk: 🟢🟡🟠⚪⚪  Volatility: 🟢🟡🟠⚪⚪

This week, overall portfolio movement was limited, with modest gains in some holdings offset by losses in others. PLD recovered slightly from the previous week, while TSLA and RHM.DE saw notable declines.
Key market events included a strong earnings call from NVDA that boosted the broader technology sector, rising oil prices following Ukrainian drone strikes in Russia, Bulgaria’s announcement that it will join the eurozone, and continued uncertainty around US trade tariffs. Political developments in Eastern Europe, including the election of Nawrocki in Poland, led me to avoid additional Eastern European exposure.
In terms of portfolio impact, NVDA’s strength supported VOO, which ended the week positive. At the same time, heightened geopolitical tensions weighed on emerging markets (VWO) and some cyclical names, while industrials benefited from increased focus on defence and machinery, particularly in Europe. BYD overtaking TSLA as the world’s largest EV maker also contributed to pressure on TSLA.
As planned, I continued diversifying into European assets, especially in financials and consumer sectors, while removing candidates that were either politically exposed or highly correlated with existing holdings. My objective remained to move closer to a standard deviation below 10% while maintaining a high but realistic expected return and Sharpe ratio.
To reduce volatility, I carried out the following transactions:

Added BNP.PA (European financials)

Added ULVR.L, DGE.L, RKT.L (European consumer sector)

Reduced GOOGL and TSLA holdings significantly, based on Solver results and their contribution to portfolio risk

Further reduced BTC to limit exposure to large price swings

title: Investment Portfolio Management
text:
Week 3

Portfolio value: 102,092.30 USD (+1.59%)
Top performer: SBUX – Starbucks Corporation (+6.43%, +383.60 USD)
Worst performer: SLNG – Stabilis Solutions, Inc. (−3.97%); RHM.DE – Rheinmetall AG (−76.41 USD)


Risk: 🟢🟡🟠⚪⚪  Volatility: 🟢🟡🟠⚪⚪

Notable events this week included protests in Los Angeles, increasingly negative commentary by Elon Musk about President Trump on X, rising tensions in Europe involving the EU, Ukraine and Russia, and upcoming US–China trade talks. Overall, these developments created a mixed backdrop of higher geopolitical risk but also pockets of sector‑specific opportunity.
Despite RHM.DE being one of the weakest performers in the portfolio this week, the broader European industrials and defence sector (reflected in NATO.L) continued to trend upwards. This suggests that investors remain interested in the theme, and that RHM.DE’s pullback may reflect profit‑taking after strong prior performance and relatively rich valuations. I decided to trim exposure to RHM.DE and lock in gains, while keeping a core position in European defence.
Within consumer cyclicals, performance diverged: SBUX rallied strongly, NKE was relatively flat, and TSLA declined. Strong consumer spending and brand strength supported SBUX, whereas concerns over Chinese supply chains and Musk’s public comments put pressure on TSLA. Bitcoin continued to show positive momentum as some investors treated it as an alternative to low interest rates, though I remained cautious given its volatility and kept exposure small.
The energy sector performed well overall, driven by higher oil prices and ongoing optimism around renewable projects. EQNR benefitted from earlier wind farm contracts, XOM moved largely in line with the broader energy market, while SLNG lagged due to its small‑cap sensitivity to news and investor preference for larger, more liquid names. I identified SLNG as a candidate for potential reduction in future weeks if underperformance persisted.
After reviewing the portfolio, I executed the following transactions:

Sold 0.4 shares of RHM.DE, following strong earlier gains and recent weakness
Sold 30 shares of SBUX, which had run up significantly and appeared overextended

These trades reduced concentration risk in recent winners and increased the cash weight back to around 10%, which I consider an appropriate buffer for this strategy.

title: Investment Portfolio Management

Week 4

Portfolio value: 102,512.62 USD (+0.41%)
Top performer: EQNR – Equinor ASA (+11.82%); XOM – Exxon Mobil Corporation (+337.95 USD)
Worst performer: SLNG – Stabilis Solutions, Inc. (−9.02%); PLD – Prologis (−151.92 USD)


Risk: 🟢🟡⚪⚪⚪  Volatility: 🟢🟡⚪⚪⚪

The week was dominated by developments in the technology and energy sectors. The outbreak of war between Iran and Israel pushed oil prices up by more than 10%, while positive outcomes from US–China trade talks supported sentiment in selected sectors. Overall, the portfolio advanced modestly, but performance across holdings was quite uneven.
EQNR and XOM benefitted significantly from higher oil prices and increased focus on energy security, though this strength did not extend to SLNG. As a smaller, more volatile company, SLNG was particularly sensitive to news and investor risk appetite, and continued to underperform, which reinforced my view that its role should remain small and potentially be reduced. BAC and V both fell this week, likely reflecting renewed concerns about US economic resilience and geopolitical risk, whereas BNP.PA in the European financial sector edged higher, supporting the case for more European diversification.
BTC declined after previously trading above 110,000 USD, which is consistent with its pattern of sharp moves following strong rallies. Nevertheless, growing interest from businesses considering BTC reserves suggests longer‑term potential, so I maintained only a small position. EUR and GBP continued to appreciate against the USD, which provided an additional tailwind to my European holdings.
No transactions were made this week, as I was broadly satisfied with the portfolio’s positioning and wanted to avoid over‑trading on short‑term news. The portfolio continued to deliver average weekly growth of around 0.6%, and I focused on monitoring the evolving situation in the Middle East and its potential impact on defence and energy assets.

title: Investment Portfolio Management
text:
Week 5

Portfolio value: 101,618.67 USD (−0.87%)
Top performer: TSLA – Tesla, Inc. (+5.94%); BAC – Bank of America Corporation (+62.37 USD)
Worst performer: GOOGL – Alphabet Inc. (−6.55%); BNP.PA – BNP Paribas SA (−275.80 USD)


Risk: 🟢🟡🟠🔴⚪  Volatility: 🟢🟡🟠🔴⚪

This was the weakest week of performance for the portfolio so far. The main driver was the escalation and then apparent easing of the war in the Middle East, which initially pushed oil and gold prices higher but then led to a sharp pullback once a ceasefire appeared likely. The resulting volatility, combined with scepticism about the US market given geopolitical uncertainty, weighed heavily on technology and energy stocks.
Tech names underperformed broadly, with GOOGL among the hardest hit. Energy stocks also declined as oil prices reversed earlier gains. BAC was one of the few bright spots, supported by the easing of tensions and a more positive outlook for the financial sector. V and GOOGL were affected both by weaker technology sentiment and, in V’s case, signs of softer consumer expenditure. Overall, anything tied to technology and defence came under pressure this week.
After a lecture on portfolio construction, I became more aware of the risk of over‑exposure to individual assets. PLD appeared overweight given its recent underperformance, while consumer‑focused stocks and US financials seemed underrepresented. I also reflected that some of my earlier additions had contributed to larger drawdowns than expected. Shorting BTC was considered but rejected, as in practice high transaction costs would make such a strategy less realistic.
Following this review, I made the following transactions:

Sold PLD, which had underperformed and was overweight within REITs

Bought NKE, V, RR.L, SBUX and additional VOO to create a more balanced portfolio

The added VOO increased exposure to a diversified, more stable core holding. NKE, V, RR.L and SBUX were chosen for their potential upside: RR.L through new defence contracts in the UK and EU; NKE, V and SBUX via expected growth in consumer expenditure and, in NKE’s case, the potential for a rebound following a slump and an upcoming earnings call.

title: Investment Portfolio Management
text:
Week 6

Portfolio value: 104,042.21 USD (+2.38%)
Top performer: NKE – Nike, Inc. (+16.88%); RR.L – Rolls‑Royce Holdings plc (+564.30 USD)
Worst performer: TSLA – Tesla, Inc. (−8.90%); XOM – Exxon Mobil Corporation (−177.30 USD)


Risk: 🟢🟡🟠🔴⚪  Volatility: 🟢🟡🟠🔴⚪

This final week was one of the strongest for the portfolio, driven largely by gains in defence and selected consumer names, particularly NKE. At the same time, TSLA and energy holdings such as XOM declined, reflecting a shift in sentiment away from some high‑beta and oil‑sensitive assets.
Politically, NATO countries signed a declaration to reduce dependence on the US for defence and to allocate 5% of GDP to defence spending by 2035, which strongly supported European defence stocks. Trade tensions remained a theme: Canada and the US briefly hit a roadblock over Canada’s digital services tax before talks resumed, while US–Japan relations deteriorated due to disagreements over auto tariffs and defence spending. These developments reinforced the case for maintaining diversified geographic exposure rather than relying too heavily on any single region.
TSLA suffered a sharp drop after President Trump threatened to cut subsidies to companies run by Elon Musk, highlighting the extent to which political news can affect individual stocks. In contrast, NKE rallied strongly after top Wall Street analysts upgraded it from Hold to Buy, citing the company’s renewed focus on e‑commerce and improved pricing strategy. The positive reaction to NKE’s turnaround plan confirmed the earlier decision to add to the position ahead of its earnings call.
Across the portfolio, most assets performed well this week, with the exception of some consumer names, energy stocks and REITs, which were weighed down by lingering regulatory and macro uncertainty. As geopolitical tensions eased, oil prices fell, putting pressure on the energy sector. Financials and defence holdings, however, saw significant gains as investors grew more confident about banks and welcomed NATO’s long‑term defence commitments. As in previous weeks, broader market performance remained closely tied to developments in AI and technology.

title: Investment Portfolio Management
text:
Final Evaluation

Final portfolio value: 105,053.26 USD (+5.05% over six weeks)
Best performer: NKE – Nike, Inc. (+7.53% over the final week; >25% over the period)
Largest $ gain: VOO – Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (+251.65 USD)
Largest % loser: SLNG – Stabilis Solutions, Inc. (around −20%)


Overall risk: 🟢🟡🟠⚪⚪  Overall volatility: 🟢🟡🟠🔴⚪

Over the six‑week period, the portfolio returned 5.05%, broadly in line with major US indices and ahead of several international markets. For comparison, the S&P 500 gained 5.30%, the Dow Jones 4.76%, the NYSE 3.76%, the Nikkei 225 6.17%, the FTSE 1.42% and the EUROSTOXX 50 fell by 2.56%. This indicates that the portfolio delivered competitive performance versus a diversified set of benchmarks, particularly given its blend of US, European and emerging market exposure.
The final portfolio composition reflected three main themes: defence, diversified US equity exposure and selective consumer and financial holdings. VOO was the largest single contributor in dollar terms, providing steady gains and broad exposure to the US market. European defence names (RHM.DE, NATO.L, RR.L) and ITA, the US defence ETF, all produced positive returns as NATO countries committed to higher defence spending and Europe moved to reduce reliance on US military support. BNDX and cash provided stability and helped manage volatility, while EUR and GBP appreciation against USD added an additional tailwind to European assets.
Among individual stocks, NKE was the standout winner, benefiting from a strong earnings call, a credible turnaround plan and an upgrade from Hold to Buy by top Wall Street analysts. SBUX, GOOGL, XOM, EQNR, BAC, V, RKT.L and VICI also delivered positive returns, each for slightly different reasons: consumer strength, progress in AI and technology, energy price dynamics, improved financial conditions and resilient business models in REITs. BTC, VWO and BNDX contributed modest but meaningful gains, with VWO’s relatively low volatility suggesting that some emerging markets remained more stable than expected despite geopolitical uncertainty.
On the downside, TSLA, SLNG, DGE.L, ULVR.L and PLD detracted from performance. TSLA’s volatility was driven largely by political news and Elon Musk’s actions, highlighting the risks of concentrated exposure to a single, high‑profile CEO. SLNG, as a small‑cap energy name, was highly sensitive to news flow and investor risk appetite; however, its weight remained low at around 0.25%, which limited the impact on overall performance. PLD was the largest loser in dollar terms, and its earlier ~7% weight in REITs proved too high given the sector’s regulatory and macro uncertainty. Reducing REIT exposure to around 5% by favouring VICI over PLD improved the portfolio’s balance.
From a risk‑return perspective, the final annual figures were:




Metric
Value




Sum of weights
0.98


Exp. return
16.18%


Std. dev.
11.14%


Sharpe
1.07




These metrics show that the portfolio exceeded the initial annualised return target of around 8%, while ending with volatility slightly above the preferred 10% threshold. The Sharpe ratio of 1.07 is respectable, especially considering the limited data window (five years) and the highly event‑driven nature of the period. Throughout the assignment, I consistently used Solver to explore higher Sharpe ratio solutions but regularly rejected allocations that concentrated too much risk in a few high‑volatility assets, instead favouring more diversified, realistic portfolios.
Qualitatively, the portfolio achieved several of my original objectives:

Diversification across asset classes (equities, bonds, REITs, cash, cryptocurrency) and regions (US, Europe, emerging markets).

Meaningful exposure to structural themes such as defence, AI and renewable energy, without over‑committing to any single narrative.

Continuous monitoring and adjustment of correlation, particularly by trimming highly correlated tech names and diversifying into European financial and consumer stocks.

There are also clear lessons for the future. First, initial allocations to REITs and some niche energy names were too ambitious given the macro backdrop, suggesting that position sizing needs to reflect both conviction and volatility. Second, while macro and political events provided useful context, not every headline should trigger trades; the best decisions often came from combining quantitative metrics (Sharpe ratio, standard deviation, correlation) with calm, medium‑term judgement. Finally, there is scope to further refine the process by:

Using longer historical data where available to improve the robustness of Sharpe ratio estimates.

Introducing practical constraints on sector and single‑stock weights from the start to avoid over‑reliance on Solver.

Testing alternative rebalancing rules (for example, only trading when volatility or weights move beyond defined thresholds).

Overall, the portfolio finished with competitive returns, a solid risk‑adjusted profile and a structure that reflects a disciplined approach to diversification, risk management and macro‑aware investing. The experience reinforced the importance of balancing data‑driven optimisation with realistic, qualitative judgement in portfolio construction.

title: Japanese Criminal Justice System
text: Cover Page

title: Japanese Criminal Justice System
text:
1. Introduction
The criminal justice system in Japan is world renowned for having extremely high conviction rates, which exceed 99% (Murai 2019), and very harsh pre-trial practises. I found this really interesting, so in this essay I will look to analyse how Japan operates very stringent pre trial conditions (such as hostage justice and interrogation procedures), and employs more lenient measures (such as suspended sentences and promoting rehabilitation). The example of the Carlos Ghosn case which was covered in the lecture is one particular case that struck me because of how it highlights how the Japanese criminal system can perform. In my home country, Ireland, I could draw some similarities with Japan through the promotion of rehabilitation, but the pre trial conditions contrast vastly with the Irish less-invasive pre trial approach. This essay will also argue that Japan’s criminal justice system, when compared with international standards, has an issue with due process and diminishing individual rights.

title: Japanese Criminal Justice System
text:
2. Overview
Japan’s criminal justice system
Japan’s criminal justice system, which is heavily influenced by a German civil law framework, focuses on maintaining societal order and safety with authority. The system involves police, prosecutors (who have significant authority over investigation, and therefore pre trial procedures) and the courts. Japan’s conviction rate, which is reported at 99.8%, shows us how prosecutors must rely on confessions (Kambayashi 2023). When being sentenced, rehabilitation is favoured, and suspended sentences are handed out for less serious criminal offences. This is done with the aim of integrating these criminals back into society with the hope that they will not commit any further crime. When it comes to analyses pre trial practises, there are clear ethical issues, as prolonged detention and intense interrogations often compromise the suspects’ rights. These actions are done with the aim of extracting a confession, which then allows the court to easily convict the suspect of a crime. To combat these issues reforms have been introduced with the hope of eradicating illegal practises. Reforms include that of creating transparency in interrogations (video and audio recording of entire process), eradicating “hostage justice” (where hostages are kept in detention rooms for prolonged periods of time), establishing and expanding the court appointed attorney system for suspects (to protect suspects and provide attorneys when requested), and improving the lay judge system (increasing public participation in the judicial system)(Japan Federation of Bar Associations 2023).

title: Japanese Criminal Justice System
text:
3. Leniency
In Japan’s criminal justice system
Japan’s sentencing procedures are notably lenient, through the fact that rehabilitation is prioritised with the use of suspended sentences. For example, in 2016 a total of 60% of convictions actually led to suspended sentences (Murai 2019). The receivers of suspended sentences would only avoid imprisonment if they followed the guidelines set by the probation conditions. This process is used to be able to rehabilitate offenders and promote social cohesion at the same time. Using figures from 2023, provided by the (World Population Review 2025), we can see that these procedures have a serious influence over Japan’s incarceration rate, which is 33 per 100,000 people (9th lowest in the world). This also has many other benefits such as the reduction of overcrowding in prisons and lowering overall costs of maintaining prisons.
Although there is a positive outlook on suspended sentences and more lenient procedures, a recidivism rate of approximately 48% may indicate otherwise (nippon.com 2025). Repeat offenders are fairly common, which indicates that rehabilitation and leniency may not be appropriately balanced in order to discourage any further crimes taking place. This has been criticised by the public and media, as the belief that suspended sentences shouldn’t be given for more serious offences like assault or robbery, which would discourage these actions and maintain public confidence in regard to safety.
When I looked into the issues of leniency further, I stumbled upon the 2023 case of Rina Gonoi. While in the Japan Self-Defense Forces Rina Gonoi suffered from continuous harassment which eventually led to experiencing sexual assault from three colleagues. Initially the three colleagues received a two year prison sentence each, which was then turned into a suspended sentence, allowing them to avoid imprisonment. Only after Rina went public with details about this assault and harassment did actual progress in this case be made, with many colleagues who partook and witnessed the crime being dismissed by the JSDF and the Ministry of Defense making public apologies to Rina (Okamoto and Gallagher 2023). This case really highlights the issues regarding leniency in the Japanese criminal system, where initially this case was dropped due to lack of evidence but was revised due to public pressure. It can also be noted that in the end the assaulters only received a two year suspended sentence for ongoing harassment over two years and sexual assault (Magramo et al. 2024). Many critics argued that leniency should have not been used in such serious cases, and that cases like this should not need social pressure to be investigated further.

title: Japanese Criminal Justice System
text:
4. Pre trial practises
In Japan’s criminal justice system
In contrast to Japan’s lenient sentencing there are some extreme pre trial procedures that take place in order to secure confessions, most notably the daiyo kangoku and hostage justice procedures. Suspects can be detained for up to 23 days without charge, with prosecutors extending detention through re-arrests on related charges. We even see in an (International 2009) report that they condemn the daiyo kangoku system as it doesn’t comply with international standards or provide persons with basic human rights.
The Carlos Ghosn case that was explained in the lectures exemplifies these concerns. Ghosn, a former Nissan executive, faced multiple re-arrests to extend detention, enduring prolonged interrogations without consistent legal representation. This high profile case drew global scrutiny, highlighting how hostage justice and confession driven procedures undermine due process. Despite calls for reform, progress remains slow, emphasising the tension between Japan’s judicial practices and international human rights standards.

title: Japanese Criminal Justice System
text:
5. Death Penalty
In Japan’s criminal justice system
One other thing that interested me in Japan was the presence of the death penalty which also shows the Japanese system’s severity. When very serious offences occur, such as murder, the death sentence may come in to play, but what is more severe is the process of conducting it. Inmates are usually only informed hours beforehand, and the families of these inmates are only informed after the execution has taken place, and inmates are usually held in solitary settings for their whole time on death row. Many international bodies criticise Japan’s death penalty, including (International 2025), and push for Japan to align with international values and abolish death sentences. It is seen as dismissing one’s basic human right to life (International 2025).
When looking deeper into this matter I also discovered the case of Iwao Hakamada who spent 45 years on death row, due to being found guilty of murder in 1968. In 2014, a judge ruled that he was innocent as evidence was “fabricated” and “tampered” with by the authorities in control of the case (International 2024). After this case, many more calls for the abolishment of this procedure were brought forward to ensure that no more lives and in particular innocent lives would be lost.

title: Japanese Criminal Justice System
text:
6. Conclusion
Japan’s criminal justice system presents a complex interplay of lenient sentencing, stringent pre-trial practices, and severe punitive measures, as exemplified by its 99.8% conviction rate, hostage justice, and death penalty. Suspended sentences, applied in 60% of convictions, promote rehabilitation and reduce incarceration rates to 33 per 100,000, yet high recidivism and cases like Rina Gonoi’s reveal public concerns over leniency for serious crimes. Conversely, pre-trial practices like daiyo kangoku and prolonged interrogations, highlighted by the Carlos Ghosn case, prioritise confessions over due process, drawing international criticism. The death penalty, with secretive executions and cases like Iwao Hakamada’s, further underscores human rights issues. Compared to international standards, Japan’s system compromises individual rights, necessitating reforms such as mandatory interrogation recordings, expanded legal access, and death penalty abolition to balance societal order with fairness, preserving rehabilitative strengths while addressing ethical shortcomings.

title: Japanese Criminal Justice System
text:

title: Trump’s Policies Through the Mundell-Fleming Model
text: Cover Page

title: Trump’s Policies Through the Mundell-Fleming Model
text:
1. Introduction
This analysis employs the Mundell Fleming model to evaluate the economic and political policies of President Donald Trump and their effects on the U.S. economy, alongside projections for future implications. Trump’s “Make America Great Again” agenda promotes tax cuts for lower and middle classes, stringent immigration enforcement, enhanced domestic production, and reduced reliance on imports, with an intention to streamline government expenditure in certain areas. However, overall government spending has risen due to increased budgets for defence and immigration enforcement. Drawing on data from U.S. departments and the DXY (US Dollar Index) for exchange rates, this study explores impacts on output, exchange rates, and trade balances, identifying potential economic benefits and challenges.

title: Trump’s Policies Through the Mundell-Fleming Model
text:
2. Policies

2.1 Trade Policies
President Trump introduced tariffs to address trade deficits and stimulate U.S. GDP through greater domestic production. A 10% tariff applies to most imported goods, with higher rates of 30% for China and 25% for non compliant USMCA goods from Mexico and Canada, boosting exports and reducing imports (TariffCheck.org July 2025). Plans to escalate tariffs on August 1st to 50% for countries without trade deals could further increase consumer prices, potentially affecting economic dynamics.


2.2 One Big Beautiful Bill Act
The One Big Beautiful Bill Act implements significant tax cuts and reallocates government expenditure, yielding mixed economic outcomes. It extends the 2017 tax brackets, eliminates taxes on tips up and social security benefits, benefiting all income groups but particularly higher earners. Despite intentions to reduce government spending, overall expenditure has increased due to substantial allocations of $170 billion for immigration enforcement and $150 billion for defence, while funding for Medicaid, education, and clean energy has declined. Proposals to lower corporate taxes to 15% and replace income taxes with tariff revenue could add more than $5 trillion to deficits, posing risks to fiscal stability.


2.3 Immigration Policies
Stricter immigration enforcement aims to enhance national security but impacts labour markets. Mass deportations, projected at 500,000 annually, and expanded border security measures have significantly reduced border encounters, contributing to lower GDP through labour supply constraints. Plans to escalate deportations to 1 million per year and introduce ideological screenings may further reduce the labour force, potentially increasing economic challenges.


2.4 Exchange Rate
The U.S. dollar has depreciated, as reflected in the DXY, driven by economic instability from rising fiscal deficits due to increased government spending on defence and immigration enforcement, coupled with declining GDP and uncertainties from aggressive tariffs. Notably, countries like China and Japan have reduced their U.S. Treasury bond holdings, with China’s holdings dropping significantly and Japan also selling bonds, signalling concerns over U.S. debt sustainability amid trade tensions. A recent U.S. Treasury auction showed notably weak demand, underscoring diminished investor confidence, which has further pressured the dollar. This depreciation has boosted export competitiveness, improving the trade balance, but it also raises inflation risks and highlights challenges to the U.S. economy’s global standing.



Figure 1: US Dollar depreciation (Yahoo Finance July 2025)

title: Trump’s Policies Through the Mundell-Fleming Model
text:
3. Mundell-Fleming Model

3.1 Assumptions
In order to accurately evaluate these policies using the Mundell Fleming model, the key assumptions must be taken into consideration.

Economy: The U.S. operates an open economy, engaging in significant international trade and capital flows, with exports and imports influenced by exchange rate changes, and domestic output levels.
Exchange Rate: The U.S. maintains a floating exchange rate, as evidenced by the DXY, allowing the U.S. dollar to adjust freely to balance trade and capital accounts, with recent depreciation enhancing export competitiveness.
Capital Mobility: The U.S. exhibits near perfect capital mobility, where capital flows don’t necessarily change due to interest rate changes, but the recent tariffs and uncertainty over the US economy has an effect, which results in a slightly upward-sloping balance of payments curve.



3.2 IS (Investment-Saving) Schedule
Variables:
Y (real income/GDP) – Although one of Trump’s objectives was to increase GDP, we can clearly see that this has actually decreased.



Figure 2: US real GDP, as a percentage change from the preceding quarter (U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis July 2025)


Leakages (S+M) and Injections (I+G+X) Imports (M) have decreased, while exports (X) have increased, decreasing the BoP deficit.



Figure 3: US trade in good with the world (U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis July 2025)


Government Expenditure (G) has also increased by 6%.



Figure 4: US Government Expenditure (U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis July 2025)


Interest rates (r) have decreased to ~4.5%, and suggestions have been made that they can be decreased even further (Reuters July 25, 2025).



Figure 5: US Interest Rates (Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System July 2025)


We must also consider that taxes have been reduced which in turn would increase net income. Another consideration must be made for expenditure, where we can see growth in the CPI has led to decreased consumer spending.



Figure 6: US Consumer Expenditure (U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis July 2025)





Figure 7: US CPI (Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System July 2025)





Figure 8: US CPI (specific categories) (Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System July 2025)


Using this data we can now find the IS schedule and work out any movement in the line.



Figure 9: IS Curve Shift; Created by Martinas Jucys Brady


Here we can see that we have found that after the implementation of Trump’s policies the IS line will move to the left, suggesting that there is a decrease in the demand for goods in the market at any given interest rate.
Now we must also consider that movements will be made in order to decrease (G) Government expenditure, further decrease the trade deficit (through increased exports), reduction of tariffs, which will inadvertently increase M and decrease the CPI, which would increase consumption levels, and an increase in (I) investment which has slumped as of late due to the uncertainty in the US economy. When considering this, predictions could be made for the IS curve to move back to the right with further reduced interest rates, indicating increased demand for goods once again.


3.3 LM (Liquidity-Money Supply) Schedule
Variables:
Transaction money demand – this is the amount of money that is used for everyday transactions, we can see that with a lower Y (income) this value will also decrease.



Figure 10: US Consumer Expenditure (U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis July 2025)


Speculative money demand – this is the amount of money held as a store of value, when interest rates are low this value is high, as interest rates can be seen as an opportunity cost.
Y (income) and r (interest rate), which can have already been covered in the IS Schedule.



Figure 11: LM Curve Shift; Created by Martinas Jucys Brady


After examining the effects of Trump’s policies on the money market, we can now assess the LM schedule. Interest rates have decreased to around 4.5%, and further reductions have been proposed. Lower interest rates increase speculative demand for money, particularly as bond yields become less attractive. This causes greater demand for liquidity, especially at lower interest rates.
However, the rise in CPI indicates increasing price levels, which reduces real money balances. If the nominal money supply remains unchanged, this effectively shifts the LM curve to the left, reflecting tighter real monetary conditions at any given income level. Additionally, with GDP decreasing, we observe a movement down along the LM curve, as lower income reduces transaction demand for money and exerts less upward pressure on interest rates.
Looking to the future, it is expected that the US will continue to lower interest rates, and thus further decreasing Y. CPI looks like it will continue to rise, due to the total uncertainty of the US market with regard to tariffs. This will further reduce the transaction demand for money, and therefore more scepticism over future GDP growth. Compared to the IS schedule, the LM schedule is seemingly much more difficult to predict, largely due to the uncertainty about decisions being made by Trump and congress.


3.4 BP (Balance of Payments) Schedule
Variables :
CA (Current Account) is found by comparing exports and imports, here we can see that the number of exports is increasing compared to imports. Thus, meaning that we can assume that the value of CA is increasing and nearing a trade surplus.



Figure 12: US trade in good with the world (U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis July 2025)


K (Capital Account) is found by the net capital inflow multiplied by the difference between domestic and foreign interest rates. With the US having an increased amount of inflow compared to outflow recently, and an interest rate which is above the global average, it leads us to assume that K has increased.



Figure 13: US Investment Positions (U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis July 2025)


We can also assume that the slope of the BP line will by slightly upward sloped, as the US operated a near perfect capital mobility. I allocated a small slope as due to Trump’s policies, where capital flows may now indirectly change interest rates.



Figure 14: BP Curve Shift; Created by Martinas Jucys Brady


By analysing this BP schedule we can see that less income leads to more capital inflows. This is further influenced by tariffs as with less income you avoid imported goods and will opt to purchase cheaper domestic products or no longer buy these products at all if they are not necessary. The BP line shifting to the right indicates in order to maintain lower capital outflows that interest rates and income must also lower.
In the future, if interest rates keep being lowered, while tariffs are still in place, it would mean income would begin to suffer drastically. This would create a scary economy to live in where in order to grow the domestic market the economy must suffer on a macroeconomic level.

title: Trump’s Policies Through the Mundell-Fleming Model
text:
4. Conclusion



Figure 15: IS-LM-BP Graph; Created by Martinas Jucys Brady


By completing this analysis and displaying an IS-LM-BP graph, we can see that while the U.S. economy is at an internal equilibrium (EQ2), it is no longer externally balanced, as EQ2 lies below the BP2 curve. This indicates that the U.S. is on a path toward a Balance of Payments surplus, largely due to increased exports, reduced imports, and higher capital inflows, all driven by protectionist trade policies and an inward focus on domestic economic performance. While these strategies may boost short-term internal stability, they have created external imbalances and uncertainty in global trade relations.
To restore full macroeconomic equilibrium, policy measures such as reducing tariffs, loosening monetary conditions, or allowing the U.S. dollar to appreciate may help reduce the surplus and rebalance capital flows, aligning the U.S. back with its external trading partners.

title: Trump’s Policies Through the Mundell-Fleming Model
text:

title: Comparing and Contrasting Digital Transformation
text: Revolut v. Ulster Bank

title: Comparing and Contrasting Digital Transformation
text:
0. Executive Summary
This paper explores the digital transformation journeys of Revolut and Ulster Bank shows the contrasting outcomes in the financial services industry. Revolut achieved rapid growth and industry leadership through a unified digital strategy, leveraging cloud technology and a culture of continuous innovation to deliver integrated, customer focused services worldwide. In contrast, Ulster Bank struggled due to fragmented change efforts, outdated legacy systems, and weak strategic alignment, which ultimately led to repeated service failures and withdrawal from key markets.
The analysis underscores that successful digital transformation requires more than technology upgrades, but it also demands strong leadership, integrated business-IT strategy, investment in scalable infrastructure and ongoing cultural change. Revolut’s experience sets a benchmark for digital agility, while Ulster Bank’s difficulties serve as a cautionary example of the risks in treating digital transformation as an isolated IT project.

title: Comparing and Contrasting Digital Transformation
text:
1. Introduction
Digital transformation has emerged as a key factor in success or failure for businesses within the financial services industry. It has reshaped how businesses operate, innovate and compete, through continuous technological advancement and maximising customer experience (Glassbox 2025). A holistic digital transformation strategy is not an optional one but one that is critical to the survival and long-term competitiveness. Frameworks have grown in complexity, legacy infrastructure is fast becoming outdated and inefficient, and constant disruptions within the market are happening due to more innovation, so companies must tread carefully when implementing them. Digital transformation not only allows new entrants such as fintechs to disrupt the established market dynamics and grow a customer base through smarter, faster and more efficient services, but it also exposes the vulnerabilities and inefficiencies that traditional banking institutions have (Schwertner 2017); (Rajan 2024).
This essay critically examines the contrasting digital transformation journeys of both Revolut and Ulster Bank. These two companies show that stark contrasts in the action taken to administer digital change and outcomes when this digital change is successful and a failure within the same sector. Revolut (founded in 2015) introduces the concept of technologically driven disruption that takes advantage of new technologies, cloud infrastructure and a new business culture of rapid innovation to redefine what banking services can be (Revolut 2025). On the other hand, we have Ulster Bank (founded nearly 200 years ago) offers an insight into what can happen when a company takes a half-hearted, fragmented approach to change that is necessary. This is highlighted through Ulster Bank’s conservative approach to, strategic misalignment and a reluctance to break free from legacy systems (Weston 2013); (Doyle 2021).
By using relevant theoretical frameworks, such as the Strategic Alignment Model (Venkatraman, Henderson, and Oldach 1993) and the Five Building Blocks of Digital Design (Ross, Beath, and Mocker 2019), to closely analyse, compare and contrast the actions taken, outcomes received and lessons learned from successful and failed digital transformation procedures in the financial services industry. This essay will encompass the theories of why digital transformation is relevant to this industry and by using the examples of Revolut and Ulster Bank the reasons as to why a holistic approach with strong leadership is necessary (Chanias 2017). Then a comparative analysis will unveil the internal and external influences that led to their different outcomes. Finally, a summary of critical insights and practical recommendations will be made to companies undertaking similar transformative journeys.

title: Comparing and Contrasting Digital Transformation
text:
2. Digital Transformation at Revolut
Revolut was founded in 2015 and quickly emerged as a global fintech leader with a mission to “simplify all things money”. From their launch, Revolut quickly expanded their services to areas that include mobile banking, foreign exchange, payments, stocks and cryptocurrency. This included all aspects of banking, from spending and saving to investing and borrowing. It serves over 65 million customers across over 160 countries and currently supports 36 currencies. Revolut strives to make money management, bank transfers and investing accessible and seamless across borders. Regulation from European financial authorities has allowed Revolut to establish itself as a trusted financial service that provides a user-friendly digital platform to manage all your finances. With constant expansions into new markets such as the Australia in 2019, US and Japan in 2020, and Brazil in 2023 has allowed Revolut to disrupt the traditional banking industry through its digital first approach (Revolut 2025). Fast paced innovation, robust expansion and agile technology adoption has allowed Revolut to reinforce its position as a global fintech leader that allows customers to take control of their financial lives through the “first global financial super app” (Google Cloud 2025).
Revolut’s objectives of digital transformation are based on the revolutionising how customers manage their money by taking advantage of technology. The aim of delivering a comprehensive digital banking experience that incorporates all financial needs, empowers users with real time control over their finances through data driven insights and AI customisation (Google Workspace 2025). These goals align with Revolut’s own mission statement of “Our global mission is for every person and business to do all things money – spending, saving, investing, borrowing, managing, and more – in just a few taps” (Revolut 2025).
In order to support the ambitious digital transformation objectives, Revolut heavily relies on cloud computing infrastructure provided by Google Cloud to enable scalability, security and resilience of operations. Revolut made use of Cloud APIs and other third-party software to automatically set up and manage their computer systems, allowing engineers to quickly launch updates or undo changes when needed. This allows horizontal and vertical scaling while maintaining operational efficiency or stability (Google Cloud 2025). Google Cloud Identity and Access Management were used to enable quick and easy access to resources while maintaining high security (Google Cloud 2025).


“As a fast-growing startup, pace of innovation is very important for us. With Google Cloud, we could build an environment where we can have continuous delivery, releasing as much as we can as quickly as possible.”
Vlad Yatsenko, CTO and Founder, Revolut


This also allowed Revolut to use Google Workspace for daily operations on a global level due to the automatic integration with Google Cloud and efficient facilitation of global collaboration between different teams (Google Workspace 2025). They also used other Google applications, such as Sheets and Forms, to gather customer feedback and insights quickly. Hence enabling fast data driven product innovation and meeting the ever-changing needs of consumers (Google Workspace 2025). Revolut didn’t have to reinvent a new way of operations but used what was already available to find an easy way to operate all aspects of their business.
The digital transformation undertaken by Revolut involved data migration and adopting the system updating automation to maximise scalability and efficiency (Google Cloud 2025). On an organisational level, the real time collaboration tools offered by Google Workspace allowed teams to collaborate remotely and improve planning and project quality. Product development cycles shortened dramatically, shown through the delivery of the Payday application in only 6 months (Google Workspace 2025). Rapid market adaptation, innovation and experimentation have allowed Revolut to respond swiftly to customer feedback reports and new market developments. This stems from the founders of Revolut who emphasised a digital first mindset in forming this challenger bank to maximise the user experience and ensured that technological initiatives were taken to strongly align operations with the strategic goals (Polasik, Widawski, and Lis 2020). These actions taken enabled Revolut to continuously meet the evolving digital finance demands at a fast and competitive pace.
Revolut’s digital transformation has brought forward explosive growth, through now processing over 150 million transactions per month and surpassing a valuation of $45 billion (Wilkes 2024). The integration of Google Services has allowed them to record zero minutes of downtime and offer customers a seamless experience (Google Cloud 2025). Originally customers used Revolut as a more travel related finance app, but now it has transitioned to becoming the primary banking platform for many customers (Llabrés et al. 2025). The features that give a competitive advantage have shifted from being large to being fast and easy to use with more innovation in smaller more extravagant ways (Rybacki 2022). Revolut has been able to capitalise on the lack of and slower innovation processes of the more traditional banks and position itself as a global fintech leader through a combination of these operations (Schwertner 2017).
By using the Strategic Alignment Model, the use of a Competitive Potential pathway is apparent (Venkatraman, Henderson, and Oldach 1993). Their advanced IT strategy of continuous innovation and development has allowed their new disruptive services to flourish. This has really reshaped customer expectations of what financial services should be. This IT led approach ensures that rapid adaptation and expansion take place to redefine the fintech landscape.
This strategic transformation aligns closely with the five building blocks that are key to a successful digital design (Ross, Beath, and Mocker 2019).

Shared Customer Insights

Revolut leveraged data to reshape their services to align with current and anticipated customer needs.

Operational Backbone

The core systems within Revolut were highly scalable, secure and reliable. Google Cloud and other Google applications were the key to this operational efficiency.

Digital Platforms

Used a variety of digital services and a highly adaptable format to ensure that quick innovation and seamless integration of new services.

Accountability Framework

Leadership within Revolut took control and defined clear objectives and pathway for the business to take. Continuous development to meet regulations globally has also maintained Revolut’s trustworthiness.

External Developer Ecosystem

The partnerships made with Google allowed them to use APIs to integrate new business ideas easily. It also allowed the business to find new, modern ways to collaborate within in order to maximise success.
The combination of these concepts ensures that Revolut’s capabilities, governance, innovation, customer satisfaction, strategy and leadership all align and facilitate strong competitiveness and growth within the fast-evolving financial services industry. This alignment also empowers them to innovate rapidly, meet rising customer expectations and redefine the industry landscape effectively.

title: Comparing and Contrasting Digital Transformation
text:
3. DIgital Transformation at Ulster Bank
Ulster Bank has been one of the main players in the Irish banking sector for more than 175 years. It was originally founded in Belfast in 1836, with its first Dublin brand being opened in 1862. Ulster Bank was heavily involved in Ireland’s property bubble in the mid-2000s, but when this bubble burst it needed a £15 billion bailout. In recent years, increased competition in Ireland and the UK has meant that Ulster Bank was met with considerable pressure to modernise its systems and push towards providing customers with a seamless experience (Campbell 2021). Despite its strong market presence, the older IT infrastructure and reliance on legacy systems became a competitive disadvantage (Organ and Stapleton 2023). Ulster Bank needed a complete revamp and overhaul of cultural approach and modern technology in order to remain relevant in the rapidly evolving financial industry (Doyle 2021). They never truly recovered from the financial crisis in Ireland, and this meant that they were unable to adapt to new technologies and compete with more modern business structures. Ulster Bank became known as an ‘expensive headache’ for their parent company (RBS), and this led to the announcement of their phased shutdown in Ireland in 2021 (Campbell 2021). Ulster Bank now operates solely in Northern Ireland, as of 2023.
The objectives set by Ulster Bank for digital transformation focused on the modernisation of core banking operations, enhancing customer experience and ensuring compliance with regulations (Kennedy 2012). Their ambition was to compete with the emerging fintech sector by offering new digital services in a fast and reliable manner (Kennedy 2010). It can be said that most, if not all, companies within the banking sector had the same or similar objectives (Olanrewaju 2014). The transformation program made by Ulster Bank had a strong emphasis on risk management, security and compliance, due to what happened with the 2008 financial crisis (Organ and Stapleton 2023). Ulster Bank sought to innovate internally and allow collaboration between technology and operations teams to support future growth and innovation (Doyle 2021). Ultimately, Ulster Bank wanted to transition from a traditional banking provider to a banking provider that was modern, digitally focused and easy to use for customers, so that they could compete with new challenger banks that were emerging (Doyle 2021); (Kennedy 2010).
In an effort to meet these objectives, Ulster Bank implemented multiple technological initiatives. They invested in upgrading its core platforms, integrating data analytics and adopting process automation for repetitive processes (Weston 2013). Some of these projects included launching an updated mobile app and online banking and using cloud-based solutions to improve efficiency and data management (Kennedy 2010). This was met with some challenges with the old legacy systems, which were mainly difficulties regarding integration (Kennedy 2012). Experimentation with big data analytics and RPA also took place in an effort to streamline fraud detection, risk 8 analysis and compliance with regulations. Despite the modernisation of their business, constant service outages and poor staff training and development hindered the process (Weston 2013); (Financial Services Union 2021). Attempts were made to use AI and cloud services, but they were incremental rather than transformational, due to a conservative approach to change that was found throughout the digital transformation process (Doyle 2021).
Ulster Bank’s transformation process included the launch of Ireland’s first free mobile banking app designed to make banking more convenient for personal customers. The bank also upgraded their online banking platform and digital self-service platforms to enhance customer experience and provide more efficient account management (Kennedy 2010). Internally, Ulster Bank initiated digital literacy and upskilling programs for staff, restructured project teams and collaborated with technology vendors to accelerate digital innovation (Doyle 2021). Concrete efforts were taken to integrate new digital tools and process automation (such as data analytics and robotic process automation) to improve fraud detection, risk analysis, and regulatory compliance (Financial Services Union 2021). IT task forces were established to address challenges with legacy system integration and fragmentation, as identified by industry analysts (Kennedy 2012). The bank continually experimented with new technologies including cloud-based solutions, but these implementations were often incremental due to complex legacy infrastructure and cautious change management (Organ and Stapleton 2023); (Oludapo, Carroll, and Helfert 2024). Overall, the primary actions taken involved product launches, vendor partnerships, internal training initiatives and technology upgrades aimed at building a modern digital platform for Ulster Bank customers.
Ulster Bank’s digital transformation showed elements from both its ambition and struggle to integrate software effectively. While they were able to launch Ireland’s first free mobile banking app, upgrade other platforms and improve the customer experience (Kennedy 2010). Their transformation process was consistently interrupted with struggles in integration and software malfunctions (Kennedy 2012). The IT meltdown at Ulster Bank in 2012 left around 750,000 customers with disrupted access for several weeks, thus highlighting the reliability issues and led customers to lose trust in their services (Weston 2013). This caused a lot of public criticism and slowed adoption rates of their services, as customers trusted other banks with faster and more technologically advanced services more (Doyle 2021). Challenger banks benefitted greatly from these failures at several banks, not only Ulster Bank (Doyle 2021). Ulster Bank’s operational costs remained high, and they were unable to overcome structural inefficiencies, so it was forced to wind down retail banking operations in the Republic of Ireland and now operates only in Northern Ireland (Campbell 2021).
By using the Strategic Alignment Model, the use of a Strategy Execution pathway can be seen (Venkatraman, Henderson, and Oldach 1993). The constant misalignment between IT investment and core business strategy is also apparent (Weston 2013). Unlike firms 9 with a competitive potential pathway, Ulster Bank’s digital initiatives were typically narrow technology upgrades or isolated platform launches, failing to integrate with a broader transformation of value delivery and operational agility (Doyle 2021).
This strategic misalignment can be seen through the analysis of the five building blocks that are key to a successful digital design (Ross, Beath, and Mocker 2019).

Shared Customer Insights

Customer insight remained fragmented, with poor adoption of digital tools and limited understanding of changing customer needs.

Operational Backbone

Core systems were unreliable and inefficient, as legacy IT infrastructure frequently failed and was not fully integrated with modern cloud platforms

Digital Platforms

The bank’s efforts focused on standalone launches like the mobile app but lacked true scalable and integrated digital infrastructure which could be found in other successful projects.

Accountability Framework

Weak governance was evident from the inconsistent execution and underinvestment in foundational systems, with leadership often failing to unite business and IT priorities (Weston 2013).

External Relationships

There were few meaningful partnerships or integrations with third party platforms, and no culture of external digital collaboration developed.
Ultimately, Ulster Bank’s case serves as a cautionary example of how not to implement digital transformation. Treating it as a IT modernisation project without real integration in the business as a whole, ultimately leads to failure. This emphasises the importance of strong leadership, strategic alignment and continuous innovation in any case of digital transformation.

title: Comparing and Contrasting Digital Transformation
text:
4. Comparative Analysis
Directly comparing the cases of digital transformation in Revolut and Ulster Bank exposes two very different approaches and outcomes, while maintaining very similar primary objectives. Revolut set out to become a key figurehead in the digital banking space, aiming to “simplify all things money” (Revolut 2025). On the other hand it is apparent that Ulster Bank had a goal to enhance customer experience and modernise their platforms, but they conducted this development in a conservative manner (Organ and Stapleton 2023); (Weston 2013).
The technique used to adopt new technologies is key differential between the success of Revolut, and the decline and eventual failure of Ulster Bank. Revolut implemented advanced cloud infrastructure and new ways of collaboration using the many Google applications (mainly Cloud and Workspace) to increase scalability, security and adaptability of their platform and business (Google Cloud 2025); (Google Workspace 2025); (Rybacki 2022). Their use of APIs allowed for fast exchanges of information and development of their platforms. In contrast, Ulster Bank was constantly marred by IT failures and slow integration between old and new technologies (Kennedy 2012); (Weston 2013).
Strategic alignment and internal actions further show this contrast. Revolut’s model exemplified business and IT integration, empowering different teams to collaborate and fostering a culture of customer-centric digital experimentation. Ulster Bank, meanwhile, faced persistent misalignment through sporadic digital projects, which often were started as IT fixes rather than part of holistic strategic change, with resistance among long serving staff to learn and adopt these new technologies and underinvestment in projects (Doyle 2021); (Weston 2013).
The root causes of Ulster Bank’s untimely failure were complex, systematic, but also preventable. Strategic misalignment caused whole digital projects to just be perceived as IT upgrades, rather than a business transformation, which in turn did not allow Ulster Bank to gain a competitive advantage and quickly adapt to market changes (Weston 2013). The leadership and internal culture of Ulster Bank was heavily influenced by risk aversion and overall staff resistance to change. It was noted that senior staff possessed a lack of enthusiasm for transformative change, which led to constant inconsistencies in the implementation and execution of new projects (Doyle 2021). The lack of enthusiasm from senior staff also partially led to the ineffective adaptation and integration of legacy systems. This led to constant digital breakdowns and ultimately put customer data at risk. Ulster Bank’s system became known as fragile and unreliable (Weston 2013); (Kennedy 2012). Teams that facilitated change became overloaded due to the failed in-house development and hiring of staff, which led to a shortage in digital talent that was available to them. Staff were ultimately unable to keep up with the integration and maintenance of new technologies, thus causing Ulster Bank to be left behind by competitors who could build an efficient business strategy for digital transformation (Doyle 2021). The bank’s governance structures were not robust enough to sustain complex transformation initiatives, leading to inconsistent project prioritisation and frequent shifts in strategic focus. Often, nobody was clearly accountable for the success or failure of digital programs, making it difficult to drive results or learn from setbacks . This weak accountability undermined stakeholder confidence, resulting in poor oversight and repeated execution failures (Weston 2013). Ulster Bank’s digital initiatives were often developed without fully understanding evolving customer needs, leading to fragmented customer insights and poor adoption of new platforms. Customers encountered ongoing challenges using digital tools due to frequent system failures, limited functionality, and unreliable service . This disconnect led to declining customer trust, increased churn, and a loss of market share to more agile challenger banks (phys.org 2012); (Kennedy 2012).
While Revolut’s story has often been described as a fintech success, its rapid growth brought significant challenges. The company faced considerable regulatory scrutiny and operational risks scaling across jurisdictions, especially as financial authorities demanded compliance in anti-money laundering and consumer protection (Polasik, Widawski, and Lis 2020); (Wilkes 2024). Managing enormous increases in user volume tested Revolut’s platform resilience, sometimes resulting in criticism around customer support (Google Cloud 2025). International expansion required ongoing attention to local market norms and talent capacity, showing that innovation and agility must be balanced with continuous investment in governance, risk management and secure operations (Ross, Beath, and Mocker 2019).
While both companies operated in similar regulatory and market environments including the rise of challenger banks and rapid fintech innovation, internal readiness determined the outcome. Revolut’s risk-taking, collaborative culture, and digital vision stood in stark contrast to Ulster Bank’s procedural inertia, compliance focus and slow decision cycles. Ultimately, Revolut’s transformation delivered explosive growth, trusted brand reputation, and business agility, whereas Ulster Bank’s cautious approach, persistent outages, and governance failures led to market contraction, reputational decline, and eventually withdrawal from the Republic of Ireland. Crisis analysis using the Five Building Blocks demonstrates Revolut’s strength in customer insight, operational backbone, platform creation, governance, and partnerships, in stark contrast to Ulster Bank’s fragmentation, poor integration, and lack of strategic cohesion.

title: Comparing and Contrasting Digital Transformation
text:
5. Recommendations
The case of Revolut clearly identifies the importance of strategic alignment between IT initiatives and core business objectives. While the case of Ulster Bank identifies what happens when you don’t take a holistic approach and don’t have a strong business model framed around integration.

Strategic Alignment

Digital projects must serve the overall business objectives, with transformation being facilitated through a strong and united leadership and workforce that want to embrace digital change (Venkatraman, Henderson, and Oldach 1993).

Cloud and Data Platforms

Investment in scalable and adaptable infrastructure is a must. Cloud platforms, API and external collaboration platforms allow a business to achieve automation, innovation and integration on a scope that could not be achieved without it. Developing partnerships with providers of infrastructure of this kind is highly advantageous (Google Cloud 2025); (Google Workspace 2025).

Customer Focused Innovation

Using customer feedback lets you quickly capture what customers actually want form your product/service. Taking note of new industry norms or trends and constantly innovating your platforms are key to maintaining customer satisfaction (Llabrés et al. 2025).
What Ulster Bank should have done to overcome their challenges:

Develop a clear roadmap for cloud adoption and legacy decommissioning, prioritising resilience and flexibility (Weston 2013).
Prioritise staff upskilling and business culture development, not just digital teams, empowering teams to drive innovation and change (Oludapo, Carroll, and Helfert 2024).
Implement accountability frameworks, with transparent, measurable delivery milestones for digital initiatives. This ensures that when issues arise, those that are accountable are easily identifiable.
Ensure regulatory compliance is built into transformation from the ground up, using DORA and other frameworks to guide ICT risk management (Shergold 2025).
Allocate realistic resources and set transition timelines that avoid customer disruption, learning from the costly rushed migration processes (Financial Services Union 2021).

Broader Industry Implications
Success in digital banking demands strong leadership, strategic alignment, resilience for regulatory compliance, agile infrastructure and cultures of learning and experimentation. Achieving sustainable business-IT alignment is not a goal that should be at a standstill but ever-developing, through the use of a continually interacting and adapting strategy, culture, skills and collaboration. Banks that treat digital transformation merely as “technology modernisation” rather than as an integrated organisational transformation will remain vulnerable to rapid market shifts (Amarilli 2024).

title: Comparing and Contrasting Digital Transformation
text:
6. Conclusion
The comparative analysis of Revolut and Ulster Bank highlights the crucial role of strategic alignment, leadership and technological integration in digital transformation. Revolut were able to integrate innovation, cloud computing and the relentless focus on increasing customer expectations. This allowed them to grasp a competitive advantage in the financial services market, ultimately reshape this market as a whole and set themselves as the benchmark for what is expected from digital banking. In contrast, Ulster Bank risk averse and conservative approach to the implementation of digital transformation constrained them from being able to compete in this modernising market. This was due to their outdated systems, business culture and fragmented approach. This analysis reinforces the idea that digital transformation must go beyond being a simple technology upgrade but as an advancement that every area of a business, from culture and strategy to actions and customer engagement, must undertake to be able to compete in today’s market and continue to grow. Modern companies must embrace a holistic innovation model by balancing technological advancement with their own adaptability and customer base.
